---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slideblue

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size50[.center[

What are the key elements of a domestic process that aims to solve environmental problems?

]]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. ...

<br>

#### Readings
1. Clarke, T., & Peterson, T. R. (2016). *Environmental Conflict Management*. SAGE Publications, Inc. **ONLY chapters 1 and 2**
2. Hughes, J. W. (2007). *Environmental problem solving: A how-to guide*. University Press of New England. **ONLY chapter 4**
3. Kraft, M. E. (2011). Environmental Policy and Politics (5th ed.). Longman. **ONLY chapter 3**



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

# The Semester: Three Sections 

.size45[
1. **How do we approach environmental politics? (4 wks)**

    + "Politics"
    + "Environment"
    + "Policy"
]

???

So far in our class we have been defining our key concepts to set us up for our work this term.

I know this has felt abstract so far this term.
- Think of it like learning a new language.
- We're learning the alphabet we'll need to get going on the work to come.

So, keep at it and this will all become more concrete as we go.

<br>

Let's zip through these to keep it all fresh.

### What is our operating definition of "politics" in this class?
+ Interests: We assume actors have preferences and pursue those preferences
+ Institutions: We assume rules matter although not always in the way they were designed
+ Interactions: We assume actors run into difficulty when they clash with other people

<br>

### Per Cronon's article, what does the "environment" refer to?
+ The wilderness is a human construct and as currently defined it is hugely destructive
    + Nature does not only exist in distant, pristine lands
    + We are responsible for it everywhere and must own that
    + Humans have always managed the wilderness, the test of "good" should not forget this.
    
SLIDE: Policy



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size60[**Policy Defined**]

.size40[
"A definite course or method of action selected (as by a government, institution, group or individual) from among alternatives and in the light of given conditions to guide and usually determine present and future decisions..." (Webster's Third International Dictionary).
]

???

### And what were the three key components of our dictionary definition of "policy"?
#### - What do we need to ensure our policy designs include?

1. A definite course or method of action
2. Compared to alternatives
3. Adapted to "given conditions"



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-1.png')
background-size: 60%
background-position: center
class: middle, slideblue

???

On Tuesday we started digging into a textbook chapter by Munger meant for training policy analysts.

Now that you've had a couple days for this to simmer let's make sure we got what we needed out of this.

### As a group of aspiring policy designers, what are the key lessons we can draw out of this figure?

1. Everyone agrees the "right" policy is the "wise" policy, but there are many types of "wisdom"
    + Experts vs Markets vs Politics
    + As you identify the stakeholders related to your chosen problem feel free to group them like this!
    
2. One way of analyzing a policy dispute is to think of it as a fight over choosing the "right" test
    + All policy fights are battles over "wisdom"

3. When two "wisdoms" collide the figure shows you the most likely reasons for the dispute and the terms in which it is likely playing out.
    + Politics vs Markets: How do we define and promote equity that benefits a community?
    + Experts vs Markets: How do we promote greater efficiency of the market to the benefit of the community?
    + Experts vs Politics: How do we reform the institutions to "improve" the function of our democracy?

<br>

### Make sense?

From this perspective all policy-making is a battle over which test of "wisdom" should be used to evaluate a policy



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-3.png')
background-size: 55%
background-position: center
class: slideblue

???

We then jumped to Munger's Figure 2.3 Three Stages of Policy Choice.

### As a group of aspiring policy designers, what are the key lessons we can draw out of this figure?

(EVERY policy you design MUST include three important arguments!)

First you MUST MAKE AN ARGUMENT that we, meaning our community, have a problem.

Second you MUST MAKE AN ARGUMENT that we should address this problem as a collective and not a private decision. AND

Finally, you MUST MAKE AN ARGUMENT that this decision is best made using democratic processes or by delegating to experts.

<br>

That's three arguments you have to make BEFORE we've even begun talking about the design of your preferred policy action!

<br>

Important for us to realize the groundwork that must be completed before policy design truly comes into play.

Solving community problems are not for the faint at heart.

HOWEVER, if you lay the groundwork appropriately, the next steps become feasible!

<br>

### Make sense?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-4.png')
background-size: 67%
background-position: center
class: slideblue

???

### As a group of aspiring policy designers, what are the key lessons we can draw out of this figure?

1. Placing a specific problem in this grid is INCREDIBLY subjective
    + Anyone who tells you something is clearly about liberty or clearly about community harms is telling you about their personal values.
    
2. This means when someone makes one of these arguments they are revealing their problem framing in terms of these two dimensions.

3. You will need different arguments to move an opponent to your plan from private to public then you will from individual to collective.
    + Figuring out an argument to move your opposition across boxes can be a big part of selling your policy

<br>

### Questions on all we've done so far?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size55[**What are the key elements of a domestic process that aims to solve environmental problems?**]]

.size40[
+ Hughes (2007)

+ Kraft (2011)

+ Clarke and Peterson (2016)
]

???

All of this brings us to our work for today.

There are many, many textbooks out there all grappling with environmental problems from a variety of perspectives.

<br>

Why these books?

+ From Hughes (2007) we get a book from an independent publisher that aims to be user friendly and accessible.

+ From Kraft (2011) we get a specialist in American public policy mapping out the policy process.

+ And from Clarke and Peterson (2016) we get an approach rooted in the communications and management literatures.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size55[**What are the key elements of a domestic process that aims to solve environmental problems?**]]

.size35[
+ DOC'S KEY approach (Hughes 2007, ch4)

+ The Policy Process Model (Kraft 2011, ch3)

+ The Collaborative Approach to Environmental Conflict (Clarke and Peterson 2016, ch1 and 2)
]

???

I've tried to pick out the chapter from each of these books that describes its central approach to our challenge.

Our job today is to extract the key elements from each of these processes so we can then combine them to construct a process we can use to solve environmental problems.

<br>

### Did any one of them speak to you personally more than the others?

#### - Why or why not?

<br>

Ok, first let's dig into each one separately.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size35[
**DOC'S KEY approach**

+ Hughes, J. W. (2007). Environmental problem solving: A how-to guide. University Press of New England.
]

<br>

.size45[.center[**What are the most important pieces of advice for environmental problem-solvers?**]]

???

Keep our prompt in your mind and take 5 minutes to reflect on the DOC'S KEY approach in Hughes (2007).

<br>

As you reflect I want you to identify the most important pieces of advice for environmental problem-solvers

+ I'm not looking for you to rewrite the process described by the chapter.

+ Instead, I want you to think about what this process tells us about environmental problem-solving at the domestic level.

+ If these are the steps that matter, then what IS the system and how DOES it work?

<br>

### Any question on this?

Get to it!

<br>

Let's keep this train rolling!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size35[
**The Policy Process Model**

+ Kraft, M. E. (2011). Environmental Policy and Politics (5th ed.). Longman.
]

<br>

.size45[.center[**What are the most important pieces of advice for environmental problem-solvers?**]]

???

The policy process described in Kraft (2011) is a bit denser, so let's take 10 minutes on this one.

+ Keep our big aim in mind!

+ Don't worry about being comprehensive, this is about extracting usable ideas and suggestions.

<br>

*After time expires*

### Everybody doing ok?

Last one!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size35[
**The Collaborative Approach to Environmental Conflict**

+ Clarke, T., & Peterson, T. R. (2016). Environmental Conflict Management. SAGE Publications, Inc.
]

<br>

.size45[.center[**What are the most important pieces of advice for environmental problem-solvers?**]]

???

Last one.

Take 5 minutes to reflect on the Collaborative Approach to Environmental Conflict by Clarke and Peterson.

+ Keep our big aim in mind!

+ Don't worry about being comprehensive, this is about extracting usable ideas and suggestions.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size55[**What are the key elements of a domestic process that aims to solve environmental problems?**]

.size35[
+ DOC'S KEY approach (Hughes 2007, ch4)

+ The Policy Process Model (Kraft 2011, ch3)

+ The Collaborative Approach to Environmental Conflict (Clarke and Peterson 2016, ch1 and 2)
]

???

** Split class into four groups **

Groups, take 15 minutes to develop an answer to this big question.

### Based on your analyses and reflections, what are the key elements of a domestic process that aims to solve environmental problems?

<br>

*ON BOARD*

### Let's go, what conclusions have we tentatively reached?

<br>

**What stood out for me?**

#### Hughes 2007
- Definition (problem and outcome; avoid letting strategies be presented as the problem; problems are neutral and don't typically need 'must' or 'should')

#### Kraft 2011
- Can be used to figure out where in the process to target different kinds of pressure. The "right" strategy depends on stage of the process
- On the other hand, are we convinced these "stages" are really so distinct? How many feedback loops? How often does something go cleanly through the process?
- In other words, how much does the end of that chapter (the complexities of the US system) put the lie to the process in the first half?

#### C&P 2016
- Makes a strong case for why a collaborative approach matters but the "required conditions" sound incredibly hard to achieve at anything but the very local level
- Required conditions for a collaborative approach (p16-18): Representation of multiple interests, voluntary participation, direct engagement, mutual agreement on process, mutual agreement on decisions

<br>

## My NOTES

DOC'S KEY approach (Hughes 2007, ch4)

- People matter and perspectives differ (even on identical events)
- Three types of problem and unstructured are the hardest to solve
- DOC'S KEY: Definition (problem and outcome; avoid letting strategies be presented as the problem; problems are neutral and don't typically need 'must' or 'should'), Objectives (set clear targets), Constraints (be aware of boundaries, limitations, assumptions, unacceptable impacts), Strategies (Brainstorm and be creative), Keepers (Choose the best strategy), Experiment (Test a strategy and adapt), Yes! (Implement)

The Policy Process Model (Kraft 2011, ch3)

Stages

1. Agenda setting (critical stage, how a problem is perceived and how public reacts to it)
2. Policy formulation
3. Policy legitimation (giving legal force to decisions AND is the action perceived as legitimate)
4. Policy implementation (Put the program into effect, fund and staff it)
5. Policy and program evaluation
6. Policy change

Chapter ends by noting some of the unique complexities of the federal system of government in the US

The Collaborative Approach to Environmental Conflict (C&P 2016, ch1 and 2)

Chapter 1: Frames environmental conflict management as a communication process (2). Environmental problem-solving requires interdisciplinary approaches (4) to produce solutions that respect the identity (4) and culture (5) of the stakeholders, accounts for scientific uncertainty (7) and the discrepancies between issues and political boundaries (8).

Chapter 2:

- Complexity and difficulty of solving env problems encourage us to use a process that includes 11 important elements (p12)
- Collaborative process should be adaptable, rely on appropriate science and tech, be implementable and have low transaction costs, has appropriate funding, is measurable, is socially legitimate
- Required conditions for a collaborative approach (p16-18): Representation of multiple interests, voluntary participation, direct engagement, mutual agreement on process, mutual agreement on decisions
- Benefits listed p18-


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size40[**1) What are the opportunities for normal people to influence this process?**]

???

## 1

### Is this an important element that is missing from our approaches? Why or why not?

--

<br>

.size40[**2) Is this process better at addressing some kinds of problems than others?**]

???

<br>

## 2

### Any big blindspots here?

#### - Some kinds of problem that don't lend themselves to easy solution?

--

<br>

.size40[**3) If this is our process, how optimistic should we be about solving environmental problems?**]

???

<br>

## 3

### What specific elements of our process are key to drawing in opposition figures?

#### - Where do we think the biggest process obstacles will be?


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-swirls.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size60[**Next Week**

The Role of Stakeholders in Environmental Problem-Solving
]

???

### Questions?
